summary:
The First Battle of Panipat (April 21, 1526 AD) was the decisive engagement at which [[event:2512|Babur]], the Timurid prince from Fergana (modern Uzbekistan), defeated the Delhi Sultan Ibrahim Lodi and established the [[event:103|Mughal Empire]], transforming the political map of northern India in a single day. Babur's force of approximately 12,000–15,000 was massively outnumbered by Ibrahim Lodi's army estimated at 100,000 with war elephants — but Babur possessed two decisive advantages: Ottoman-trained artillery (under Ustad Ali Quli and Mustafa Rumi) deployed in an innovative wagon-fort (tulughma) formation, and matchlock firearms, both technologies that the Delhi Sultanate lacked. His flanking cavalry tactics combined with artillery fire created a devastating envelopment.

Ibrahim Lodi was killed on the battlefield, effectively ending the Lodi dynasty. Babur marched to Delhi and Agra, seizing the treasury (including the Koh-i-Noor diamond, according to tradition) and establishing Mughal control of the Gangetic plain. The battle's significance extended beyond the immediate political change: it introduced artillery and firearms to Indian battlefield tactics at scale, beginning the gunpowder revolution in South Asian warfare. Babur himself, in his extraordinary autobiography the Baburnama — one of the great personal memoirs in world literature — described the campaign with remarkable candor and literary skill. Panipat itself became the recurring site of decisive battles for control of northern India: the [[event:2448|Third Battle of Panipat]] in 1761 would end Maratha dominance at the same field 235 years later.
